NOTES: This speaks to protests that occurred in Cameroon when President Biya loosened restrictions on opposition parties. While there were some evidence of ethnic tensions in the past and was even mentioned in a few of the sources, this does not seem to be the case. The only one can be ruled out is the senior partner in the Beti people. As such, I chose the primary junior partner in the protests, the Bamileke people. Though I admit this may not be the best representation.

While Djibouti is predominantly in contention between the dominant Isaa and junior partner Afar people, the Arab Spring in Djibouti was not lead by the ethnic Afar group FRUD and was instead lead by the LDDH. As such it did not seem to have an ethnic claim attached to it. It happened that the Isaas are in control and the majority looking to overthrow were Afar.

While Divers_Eth coded as 0, Guinea was shown to have two other ethnic groups aside from the Susu joining them. camp_name: Military Junta for the Consolidation of Democracy, Peace and Justice
SOURCES: EPR; Uppsala Conflict Data Program; Historical Dictionary of the Republic of Guinea-Bissau; University of Pennsylvania African Studies Center
NOTES: This conflict speaks to a coup attempt to oust President Jao Bernardo "Nino" Veiera led by Ansumane Mane after the former was accused of arms dealing and other corrupt schemes. The conflict appears non-ethnically motivated and is more political motivated. However, in EPR, after the coup ended in 1999, the Papel lost their senior partnership and became powerless. The junior partner Balanta people went from junior partners to Dominant. As such, I selected them as the ethnic group with no ethnic claim.
